# Weeknight Sourdough

A schedule-friendly sourdough method: mix Tuesday evening, bake Wednesday
evening, no daytime babysitting. Makes one 900g boule.

## Starter maintenance

Keep 50g of starter in the fridge between bakes. The Sunday before a bake
week, take it out and feed it 1:5:5 (10g starter, 50g flour, 50g water)
once daily at room temperature. By Tuesday it should double within 5 hours
of feeding and smell yogurt-tangy, not nail-polish sharp. An acetone smell
means it's starving — feed twice daily for a day before trusting it.

## Formula

- 450g bread flour
- 50g whole wheat flour
- 360g water (72% hydration)
- 100g ripe starter
- 10g salt

## Tuesday evening: mix and bulk

Autolyse the flours and 340g of the water for 45 minutes. Add starter and
the remaining 20g water, pinch through, rest 20 minutes, then add salt.
Four sets of stretch-and-folds at 30-minute intervals, then leave the bulk
fermentation to run overnight at cool room temperature (18–19°C). Target is
roughly 60–70% volume increase by morning — jiggly, domed, bubbles at the
edges.

Shape Wednesday morning before work: preshape round, bench rest 20 minutes,
final shape tight, then into a banneton and straight to the fridge for a
cold proof until evening.

## Wednesday evening: bake

Bake from cold. Dutch oven preheated at 250°C for 45 minutes; score, load,
lid on. 20 minutes lidded at 250°C, then 22–25 minutes uncovered at 230°C
until deeply browned. Internal temperature should pass 96°C. Cool a minimum
of 90 minutes — cutting early ruins the crumb you waited two days for.

## Troubleshooting

### Dense, gummy crumb

Nine times out of ten this is underproofed bulk, not a bad starter and not
the bake. Cool overnight kitchens run slower than recipes assume: if the
morning dough shows little volume gain and no edge bubbles, give it 2–3
more hours warm before shaping instead of pushing on. The other common
cause is cutting the loaf warm — gumminess from early slicing disappears
once the loaf fully cools. Only after ruling those out, look at hydration:
drop water to 70% and re-test.

### Flat loaf, no oven spring

Usually overproofed (the overnight ran too warm) or a weak final shape.
Move bulk somewhere cooler, or cut bulk short at 50% rise; tighten the
final shape until the surface resists a poke.

### Pale crust

Your oven loses heat at load time. Preheat longer, load faster, and
consider baking the first 10 minutes 10 degrees hotter than the recipe.
